Looking carefully at `teams-live-recall-utterance.ts` lines 818-819: `decisionReason = cooldownClear ? 'rate_cap_reached' : 'cooldown_active';` — this is in the `else` branch where `cooldownClear && rateCapClear` is false. If `cooldownClear` is true, then `rateCapClear` must be false, so the reason should be rate cap. That's correct logic.

Let me check the `existsByName` duplicate handling and one more thing — the `findChannelForClaimPair` and `oneHourAgo` interjections.

I have enough to compile a thorough findings report. Let me note one thing about the lull-interjection cron racing with itself.

Here is my complete review.

---

# Code Review: Trigger.dev Workers (`apps/workers/`)

I read every file under `apps/workers/src/trigger/`, `apps/workers/src/lib/`, and `trigger.config.ts`. Findings grouped by file, with the recently-reworked `teams-live-recall-utterance.ts` examined most closely.

## `teams-live-recall-utterance.ts`

**1. HIGH — Rate-limit / cooldown check is computed once, never re-checked inside a transaction; concurrent utterances bypass the per-hour cap.**
Lines 731-740, 802-816. `channelRateState()` reads the last intervention + count, then the code makes a model call (seconds long) and only afterward inserts the `oracleInterventions` row. There is no advisory lock, no unique constraint, and no re-check. Recall delivers finalized utterances rapidly; this task is triggered per-utterance with no concurrency guard, so N utterances arriving within the same window all read `inLastHour < max` simultaneously and all post. `MIN_SECONDS_BETWEEN_LIVE_QUESTIONS` (20s) is likewise advisory only. Result: the meeting can get spammed past `maxInterjectionsPerHour`. Fix: gate posting behind a `pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(channelId))` transaction that re-reads `channelRateState` after acquiring the lock and inserts the intervention in the same tx, or add a partial unique index / time-bucket constraint.

**2. HIGH — Prompt-cache breakpoint is never set, and the cacheable system block is followed by an uncached block that includes the system prompt's own purpose — but the real cache problem is the duplicated dynamic content.** Lines 477-515. The `userContent` block (`utterances`) re-embeds the retrieved-claims text region indirectly and the full rolling transcript window on every single utterance. Only the `sys` block is marked `cacheEligible: true`; the retrieved-claims block is `cacheEligible: false` and is placed *between* the stable system block and the dynamic block. That ordering is correct (stable first), but: (a) the retrieved-claims block changes every utterance (retrieval is re-run per utterance), so nothing past the system prompt is cacheable; (b) there is no explicit cache breakpoint after the system block. For a task that fires many times per meeting on the same Haiku route, the static `LIVE_ORACLE_SYSTEM` (~1.5KB) should carry a cache breakpoint so it is reused across utterances within the 5-min window. As written, every utterance pays full input-token price on the system prompt. Fix: set a cache breakpoint on the `sys` block (the Anthropic adapter supports `cacheEligible`, but verify a breakpoint is actually emitted — only one block being eligible with no other cached prefix means the cache write may never amortize). Consider also caching the retrieved-claims block keyed by claim-set hash rather than re-retrieving every utterance.

**3. MED — Retrieval is run on every utterance that passes `isWorthModelPass`, including duplicates of recent retrievals.** Lines 471-475, 384-387. `buildLiveRetrievalContext` runs `searchWithRetrievalPlan` + `loadEvidenceForClaims` + section lookup + brain snippets — 4 DB round-trips plus an embedding — for each qualifying utterance, with no caching across the rolling window. Within one meeting, consecutive utterances on the same topic re-retrieve nearly identical claim sets. Fix: cache the retrieval result keyed by a normalized hash of `stripOracleAddress(utterance)+recentContext`, or debounce retrieval to once per N seconds per channel.

**4. MED — On the model-call catch path, `retrievalContext` is dropped (`retrievalContext: null`) even though retrieval succeeded.** Lines 636-640. When the model call throws, the returned object hardcodes `retrievalContext: null`, but the retrieval that ran earlier (line 471) produced a real context. The caller (line 752) then records `retrievalContext = null`, losing `retrievedClaimIds` / observability for a run where retrieval actually happened and cost money. Fix: return the already-computed `retrievalContext` in the catch branch instead of `null`.

**5. MED — `firstOffsetMs` returns 0 when the first word has no relative start, collapsing the idempotency key for distinct utterances.** Lines 159-162, 164-173. The `clientMessageId` includes `offsetMs` and a 12-char hash of the text. If two genuinely different finalized utterances from the same participant both have `offsetMs=0` (missing timestamps) and — far less likely but possible — the same text hash prefix, they dedupe. More practically, the offset of 0 means the dedupe degrades to (botId, transcriptId, participantId, text-hash). That's acceptable but the `offsetMs` should fall back to `end_timestamp` or a word index to stay distinct. Low-to-med; flagging because the dedupe key is load-bearing for idempotency.

**6. LOW — `loadLiveSettings` runs even when the utterance is short/irrelevant.** Line 729: `loadLiveSettings(db)` is awaited before the `isWorthModelPass(text)` short-circuit is fully exploited — actually it is read before the `if (liveSettings.forceModelPass || isWorthModelPass(text))` check, so every utterance (including 1-word filler) costs a settings SELECT. Minor; move the cheap `isWorthModelPass(text)` gate before loading settings, and only load settings when `forceModelPass` could matter (which itself requires the settings read — so instead cache settings per process with a short TTL).

**7. LOW — `decideLiveQuestion` inserts the `oracleContextPacks` row (line 517) before the model call even when the call will be skipped for empty content; fine here, but the failure-path model_run insert (line 609) omits `inputHash`** that the success path sets — inconsistent observability rows. Cosmetic.

## `recall.ts`

**8. MED — No retry / status-class handling on `send_chat_message`; a transient 429/5xx throws and bubbles to fail the whole task after the message was already persisted.** Lines 17-32. In `teams-live-recall-utterance.ts` the assistant `messages` row and `oracleInterventions` row are inserted *after* `sendRecallChatMessage` succeeds (line 771 before 772), so a send failure correctly avoids the DB rows — good ordering. But the throw propagates to the outer catch (line 844) and marks the entire `job_runs` row failed and *re-throws*, which triggers Trigger.dev's retry (maxAttempts 3). On retry the whole utterance reprocesses: the dedupe at line 683 catches the message re-insert, returns `skipped: duplicate`, and the question is never sent. So a transient Recall hiccup permanently drops that interjection. Consider catching the send error, logging, and completing the job without re-throw (the utterance message is already saved).

## `lull-interjection.ts`

**9. HIGH — Cron runs every minute with no overlap guard; `decideLullInterjection` reads rate state outside any lock, same race as #1.** Lines 568, 201-224, 523-532. The minute cron can overlap a prior slow run (model drafting on many channels), and two runs (or this + the contradiction watcher + the recall worker, which all write `oracleInterventions` for the same channel) can both pass the cooldown/rate-cap gate and double-post. There's no advisory lock around the gap→ask→insert sequence. Also, gap status is flipped to `'asked'` only after drafting (line 535), so two overlapping runs both select the same open gap (line 262) and both draft+post it. Fix: advisory lock per channel, or `UPDATE gaps SET status='asked' ... WHERE id=? AND status='open' RETURNING` as an atomic claim before drafting.

**10. MED — `loadChannelContext` issues 5 sequential queries per channel, and the task loops channels sequentially.** Lines 184-289, 602-617. For each active channel: last-user-msg, last-intervention, hour-count, participant list, gap candidates (LIMIT 50), typing — 6 round-trips, all awaited serially, and channels processed one at a time. With many active channels this is slow on a 1-minute cron. The gap-candidates query (line 238, LIMIT 50 ordered by CASE+createdAt) is also re-run per channel though open gaps are global; it could be fetched once per run and filtered in memory by participant. Fix: hoist the open-gap fetch out of the per-channel loop; parallelize the independent per-channel reads with `Promise.all`.

**11. LOW — `recentMessageExcerpts` uses `lastUserMsgRows` ordered DESC then `.reverse()`, but `secondsSinceLastUserMessage` uses `lastUserMsgRows[0]`** (the newest) — correct, just noting the dual-purpose query is fine.

## `contradiction-watcher.ts`

**12. HIGH — The pair-existence dedupe query is wrong and will both miss real duplicates and match unrelated rows.** Lines 418-427. The intent is "does a contradictions row exist for this unordered pair (claimId, candidate.id)?" The query uses `claimAId IN [claimId, candidate.id] AND claimBId IN [claimId, candidate.id]`. This matches a row where `claimAId=claimId, claimBId=claimId` (a self-pair, impossible here) but more importantly it matches **any** row whose A and B are both drawn from the two-element set — including a row `(claimId, X)` is *not* matched (good), but a pre-existing `(candidate.id, claimId)` reversed pair *is* matched (good). The real bug: it also matches `(claimId, candidate.id)` and `(candidate.id, claimId)` — intended — but will **falsely match** if another row happens to be `(claimId, candidate.id)` from a different detection… that's the same pair, fine. The actual defect is it does **not** constrain A≠B nor that the two columns are the *specific* pair; e.g., a row `(claimId, claimId)` would match, and if claim X later forms pairs, a row `(claimId, someOther)` won't match (correct). On balance the `IN/IN` form is loose but mostly works; however it misses the case entirely when there are 3+ contradictions involving `claimId` — a row `(claimId, Z)` with Z≠candidate is excluded (correct) — so the concrete issue is it can match a row that pairs claimId with itself or pairs the two claims with a *third* via separate rows. Recommend the explicit form: `(claimAId=claimId AND claimBId=candidate.id) OR (claimAId=candidate.id AND claimBId=claimId)`. At minimum this is fragile; med-to-high.

**13. MED — N+1 adjudication and per-candidate existence query inside the candidates loop.** Lines 416-577. For each ANN candidate: one existence SELECT, one model call, then (if contradiction) channel lookup + last-intervention + hour-count + inserts. The existence checks could be a single batched query before the loop. More importantly, each adjudication is a separate LLM call with the same `CONTRADICTION_ADJUDICATION_SYSTEM` prompt — the stable system block is `cacheEligible: true` (line 184) but, as in #2, no cache breakpoint is guaranteed and the pair content differs every call, so the system prompt isn't amortized across the up-to-8 candidates in this same run. Fix: ensure the adjudication system prompt sets a cache breakpoint so the 8 sequential calls reuse it.

**14. MED — `detectionConfidence` is hardcoded to the threshold constant, defeating the confidence gate.** Lines 480, 503, 570. `decideContradictionInterjection` is passed `detectionConfidence: CONTRADICTION_LIVE_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD` — i.e. exactly the threshold, every time — rather than a real model-derived confidence. The LLM returns `severity` but no numeric confidence, so the "confidence ≥ threshold" check is a tautology (always passes). The stored `contradictions.detectionConfidence` is therefore meaningless. Either derive a confidence from the model (add to `ContradictionCheckSchema`) or document that severity is the only signal and drop the confidence plumbing.

**15. LOW — Sweep triggers per-claim tasks in a loop with `await tasks.trigger` sequentially (line 654).** 50 sequential trigger RPCs. `tasks.batchTrigger` would be one call. Minor.

## `claim-extraction.ts`

**16. MED — Messages are marked `'processing'` (line 196) but never reset to `'pending'` on a fatal pre-segment crash, orphaning them.** If the worker dies between line 199 (mark processing) and segment processing (or the process is killed mid-run), those messages stay `'processing'` forever — no sweep resets them (unlike document-ingestion, which has a sweep covering `'processing'`). The 4-hour cron only selects `'pending'`. Fix: add a reaper that resets stale `'processing'` messages older than N hours back to `'pending'`, or wrap the claim in the segment so partial failures revert.

**17. LOW — `groupIntoSegments` re-wraps `new Date(m.createdAt)` repeatedly** (lines 983, 991) on values already typed as `Date`; harmless but redundant allocation in a hot path.

**18. LOW — Extraction blocks don't set `cacheEligible` on the stable system prompt.** Lines 311-317. The `extraction-system` block omits `cacheEligible`, relying on default. For Vertex the explicit cache is handled via `providerOptions.cache` (line 377+), but for the Anthropic fallback route there's no prompt-cache breakpoint on the large `EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT`, so fallback runs pay full price. Set `cacheEligible: true` on the system block for consistency with the other workers.

## `claim-extraction-batch-submit.ts`

**19. LOW — Empty-segment messages are marked `'skipped'` (line 173) before the batch is confirmed submitted; if a later segment throws pre-submit, the rollback (line 324) only reverts `'processing'`→`'pending'`, leaving the skipped ones stuck as `'skipped'`.** They won't be re-processed. Minor (they were user-message-free), but technically those messages are lost to extraction if the run later aborts and a different grouping would have included them. Acceptable given they had no user messages.

## `claim-extraction-batch-drain.ts`

**20. MED — `promptVersion: undefined` written to `model_runs` for batch results loses the prompt version.** Line 305. The sync path records `EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION`; the drain path sets it `undefined`. Cost/version dashboards can't attribute batch runs to a prompt version. The submit task stored the plan/version in the context pack, so it's recoverable, but the `model_runs` column is null unnecessarily. Fix: pass `EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION` (import already available pattern).

**21. LOW — `segment` is cast `as unknown as FormattedMessage[]` (line 273) but the SELECT returns `authorName` possibly null and lacks the `employeeId`-on-FormattedMessage shape that `processSegmentOutput`'s evidence insert reads via a second `segment.find` cast (claim-extraction.ts line 642).** Works because the row carries `employeeId`, but the double-cast is fragile. Cosmetic.

**22. LOW — `_settingsTableRef = settings` (line 386) is a dead export to satisfy the build;** indicates the `settings` import is unused — should just be removed rather than re-exported.

## `brain-synthesis.ts`

**23. MED — Post-validation mutations (sectionClaims, gaps insert/resolve) run as separate un-transactioned statements after `currentVersionId` is already updated.** Lines 550-586. If the process dies after `update(brainSections).set currentVersionId` (line 550) but before the gap/sectionClaims loops, the new version is live but its membership and gaps are partially applied, with no rollback. The header comment claims "Two-step transactional insert (spec 6.7)" but these statements aren't in a transaction. Fix: wrap version-insert + currentVersionId update + sectionClaims + gaps in one `db.transaction`.

**24. MED — `claimsAdded` / `resolvedGaps` loops are N+1 sequential writes** (lines 556-586). Batch the `sectionClaims` inserts (`values([...])`) and the gap inserts; resolve gaps with a single `inArray` update.

**25. LOW — `resolvedGaps` update (line 581) doesn't verify the gap belongs to this section or is currently open** — the model can resolve any gap by UUID, including gaps from other sections, with no guard. Add `AND sectionId = ?` / status check.

**26. LOW — Comment at line 379 references "Vercel AI SDK generateObject with temperature=0.1" but the repo's stated invariant (AGENTS.md) is that inference uses raw provider adapters, not the Vercel AI SDK.** Stale/misleading comment; flag for doc accuracy.

## `document-ingestion.ts`

**27. MED — Quote matching uses `chunk.text.includes(exactQuote)` to pick the evidence chunk (line 483); but chunks overlap by 150 chars, so a quote spanning a chunk boundary matches no chunk and silently falls back to `insertedChunkIds[0]` (line 484), then fails quote validation against the wrong chunk.** The claim is rejected even though the quote is genuinely in the document. Fix: search the full `rawText` for the quote and map the offset back to the covering chunk, or validate against `rawText` and attach the nearest chunk as evidence pointer.

**28. MED — `embedMany` over all chunks is one call (good), but chunk inserts are sequential N+1** (lines 251-275), one INSERT per chunk with per-row try/catch. For large documents this is slow. Batch with a single multi-row insert + `onConflictDoNothing`, collecting returned ids.

**29. LOW — `MAX_DOCUMENT_TEXT_CHARS = 15_000` truncation (line 286) means claims can be extracted only from the first ~15K chars, but evidence chunks were created for the whole document.** A quote the model "sees" is bounded by the truncated prompt, so this is internally consistent, but large docs silently lose all content past 15K from extraction. Worth a documented note / gap emission.

**30. LOW — `fileBackedCachePath` temp file is unlinked on the model-error path (line 469) and success path (line 754) but NOT if `processDocument` throws between materialize (line 195) and the try block** (e.g., the `embedMany`/chunk section throwing). The temp file leaks. Wrap in `finally`.

## `taxonomy-reclassification.ts`

**31. MED — Each proposal handler performs multiple mutations with no transaction; a mid-handler failure leaves half-applied state and no change-log row.** E.g. `handleMergeSubTopics` (lines 245-260): moves claims, deletes leftovers, retires source — three statements. If the delete fails after the move, source is not retired and no `taxonomy_change_log` 'applied' row is written, so `findPendingReclassifications` will re-select it and re-run the move (idempotent-ish, but the retire/delete ordering isn't). Wrap each `applyProposal` body in `db.transaction`.

**32. LOW — `handleReassignClaims` / `handleMergeTopDomains` fetch `alreadyInTarget` as a full claim-id list then use `notInArray(skipIds)`** (lines 195-210, 321-339). For a domain with many claims this materializes a large id array into SQL. A `NOT EXISTS` subquery or `INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING`-style move would scale better. Minor at current data sizes.

## `taxonomy-reevaluation.ts`

**33. MED — `Math.random()`-seeded k-means with no fixed seed makes proposals non-deterministic across runs**, so the same claim corpus can yield different clusters/proposals each weekly run, and the novelty check (0.88) won't reliably suppress re-proposing the same cluster. Combined with no dedupe against existing *pending* `create_sub_topic` proposals, the queue can accumulate near-duplicate proposals. Fix: dedupe new proposals against pending proposals' centroids too (currently only checks `knowledgeSubTopics`, line 261), and/or seed the RNG.

**34. MED — `nameCluster` parses JSON out of free text via regex `/\{[\s\S]*\}/` (line 195) instead of using `runObject` with a schema.** This is exactly the suboptimal-AI pattern: a structured-output task done as `runText` + brittle regex JSON extraction, defeating the adapter's native structured-output + validation. Use `client.runObject` with a Zod schema (`{name, purpose}`). Also the LLM call has no `cacheEligible` on the stable system block, so each cluster-naming call across all domains re-pays the system prompt.

**35. LOW — `processReadyDomain` loads up to 500 claim embeddings (1536-dim floats each) into memory and runs k-means in JS** (lines 234-257). For all ready domains in one run this is memory-heavy (~3MB/500 claims/domain, plus 30 iterations of O(N·k·dim)). Acceptable now; flag for scale. Also `memberClaims` sort (line 285) mutates `cluster.memberIndices` in place via `.sort()`, but it's not reused — harmless.

**36. LOW — The two task variants share `id: 'taxonomy-reevaluation'` (scheduled) and `'taxonomy-reevaluation-manual'`** but the file header and the reclassification worker both refer to "the taxonomy re-evaluation worker" singularly — fine, just confirming no id collision (there isn't).

## `teams-transcript-ingestion.ts`

**37. MED — Idempotency check (lines 136-141) and the bulk insert (line 283) are not atomic; two notifications for the same transcript can both pass the `already` check and both insert full message sets, creating a duplicate channel + duplicate messages.** `clientMessageId` `teams:<transcriptId>:<i>` would collide on a unique index if one exists, but the code relies on the pre-check, not a constraint, and it creates a *new channel* every run (line 239) so the messages land in different channels and don't collide. Fix: dedupe on transcriptId at the channel level (unique on raw_transcripts.transcript_id exists — line 256 — but the channel/messages are created before that insert), or claim the transcript atomically first.

**38. LOW — `mergeBySpeaker` concatenates cues but discards the merged cues' offsets;** the merged utterance keeps only the first cue's `offsetMs` (line 98 spreads the first `c`). Fine for ordering, noted.

**39. LOW — Bootstrap employee creation (lines 216-232) runs per distinct speaker sequentially with a SELECT-after-conflict fallback; N+1 but bounded by speaker count.** Acceptable.

## `graph-transcripts.ts`

**40. MED — `cachedToken` is module-global and shared across concurrent invocations with no locking; not a correctness bug (token is valid) but multiple parallel cold calls each hit the token endpoint.** Minor. More notably, `ensureAdhocSubscription` lists subscriptions and picks the first match but never removes duplicate subscriptions — if two ever get created (e.g., race between the 30-min cron and a webhook lifecycle trigger, both seeing `mine` absent), you get two live subscriptions and double notifications. The function is described as "ensure exactly one" but doesn't enforce uniqueness. Fix: after create/renew, delete any other subscriptions matching `ADHOC_RESOURCE + notificationUrl`.

**41. LOW — `listDisplayNameToEmail` breaks the pagination loop on any non-OK page (line 229) returning a partial map silently;** a 429 on page 2 yields half the directory, causing some speakers to fall back to name-match unexpectedly with no signal. Log the partial-page failure.

## `trigger.config.ts`

**42. LOW — `retries.default.maxAttempts: 3` applies to all tasks including `lull-interjection` (every-minute cron) and `teams-live-recall-utterance`.** For the recall utterance task, retries re-run the whole utterance and (per #8) silently drop the interjection on the dedupe path. For per-minute lull, a retry can stack on the next scheduled run. Consider `retries: { maxAttempts: 1 }` on the live/cron tasks where re-execution is harmful. Cron tasks generally shouldn't retry.

---

## Cross-cutting themes
- **Concurrency / rate-limit races (#1, #9, #12, #37):** Multiple workers write `oracleInterventions` per channel and gate on read-then-write without locks or constraints. This is the highest-impact class — the per-hour interjection cap and gap-asked transitions are not actually enforced under concurrency.
- **Prompt caching not exploited (#2, #13, #18, #34):** The repeated stable system prompts (live decision, contradiction adjudication, cluster naming, extraction-fallback) are marked `cacheEligible` inconsistently and never carry a guaranteed cache breakpoint, so high-frequency calls (per-utterance, per-candidate, per-cluster) re-pay the system prompt every time. The Vertex explicit-cache path is wired for extraction/synthesis docs, but the Anthropic/interview-route calls have no prefix caching.
- **Non-transactional multi-write sequences (#23, #31):** brain-synthesis and taxonomy-reclassification apply several dependent mutations outside transactions, risking half-applied state.
- **AI structured output done as text+regex (#34):** cluster naming should use `runObject`.
- **N+1 / sequential DB and trigger calls (#10, #13, #15, #24, #28):** several loops issue per-row queries or per-item `tasks.trigger` where batching is available.

No edits were made. The most urgent items to address first are #1/#9/#12 (interjection cap races) and #27 (document quote-chunk mismatch silently rejecting valid claims).

8	
9	Your ultimate goal is to map the "dark matter" of this company: informal rules, hidden bottlenecks, missing context in handoffs, undocumented workarounds, system limitations, and operational dependencies across all departments.
10	
11	You are not a task manager. You are not here to assign blame. You are here to understand how the company actually works.
12	
13	PERSONALITY:
14	- You are a highly intelligent, intensely curious, clinical-when-analyzing Chief Operating Officer.
15	- You are warm and friendly without being long-winded.
16	- You are empathetic but focused on operational reality.
17	- Your tone is concise.
18	- Ask one tightly scoped question at a time.
19	
20	INVESTIGATIVE TACTICS:
21	
22	1. ROOT CAUSE RULE:
23	If an employee mentions a problem, pull the thread to find the systemic root cause.
24	Example: If a handoff fails, ask what specific system field, document, meeting, person, or approval was supposed to facilitate it.
25	
26	2. SYSTEM VS. REALITY RULE:

38	If a question could sound accusatory, rephrase it around missing information, unclear ownership, or system limitations.
39	When discussing something another employee said, anonymize it unless explicitly permitted.

41	
42	5. GROUP CHAT RULES:
43	Do not interrupt human-to-human conversation unnecessarily.
44	Only interject proactively if:
45	- A major contradiction is detected, or
46	- There is a natural lull, no one is typing, and there is a high-priority knowledge gap relevant to the recent topic.
47	If directly mentioned, respond immediately using your tools.
48	
49	OUTPUT CONSTRAINTS:

// Master Oracle system prompt — spec Part 10 VERBATIM.
// Do not edit without coordinating with the spec. If you change the text,
// bump ORACLE_SYSTEM_PROMPT_VERSION and log the change in DECISIONS.md.

export const ORACLE_SYSTEM_PROMPT_VERSION = '1.0.0';

export const ORACLE_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are the "Operations Oracle" for POP Creations / Spruce Line, a high-volume home decor company.

Your ultimate goal is to map the "dark matter" of this company: informal rules, hidden bottlenecks, missing context in handoffs, undocumented workarounds, system limitations, and operational dependencies across all departments.

You are not a task manager. You are not here to assign blame. You are here to understand how the company actually works.

PERSONALITY:
- You are a highly intelligent, intensely curious, clinical-when-analyzing Chief Operating Officer.
- You are warm and friendly without being long-winded.
- You are empathetic but focused on operational reality.
- Your tone is concise.
- Ask one tightly scoped question at a time.

INVESTIGATIVE TACTICS:

1. ROOT CAUSE RULE:
If an employee mentions a problem, pull the thread to find the systemic root cause.
Example: If a handoff fails, ask what specific system field, document, meeting, person, or approval was supposed to facilitate it.

2. SYSTEM VS. REALITY RULE:
Always look for the delta between the official process and the real process.
If someone uses a personal spreadsheet, ask why they had to build it and what exact data the official system fails to show.

3. DEPENDENCY RULE:
If the conversation touches another department, follow it.
Ask how the handoff occurs, what system mediates it, and what information tends to get lost.

4. PSYCHOLOGICAL SAFETY RULE:
Do not make employees feel blamed.
Investigate systems, handoffs, unclear ownership, missing context, and system limitations — not personal failure.
When employees disagree, treat disagreement as evidence of process ambiguity, not as someone being wrong.
If a question could sound accusatory, rephrase it around missing information, unclear ownership, or system limitations.
When discussing something another employee said, anonymize it unless explicitly permitted.
Use neutral process language.

5. GROUP CHAT RULES:
Do not interrupt human-to-human conversation unnecessarily.
Only interject proactively if:
- A major contradiction is detected, or
- There is a natural lull, no one is typing, and there is a high-priority knowledge gap relevant to the recent topic.
If directly mentioned, respond immediately using your tools.

OUTPUT CONSTRAINTS:
- Never ask more than one question in a single message.
- Validate valuable answers briefly before moving on.
- Do not write essays to employees.
- Prefer sharp operational questions over summaries.`;

content.content:
1	// Claim extraction system prompt — spec Part 9.4, 9.5 + R5.5 + R12-prompt.
2	// Used by the Trigger.dev claim-extraction worker and the document-ingestion worker.
3	//
4	// Version 2.0.0 (this revision) adds:
5	//   - sensitivityFlags — strict-mode HR/PII/personal-conflict detection so the
6	//     candidate-before-claim sensitivity gate can actually fire in production
7	//     (P1 #2 from the external code review).
8	//   - proposedEntities — model-surfaced entity references (customers, licensors,
9	//     factories, systems, etc.) so R5.5's licensor-vs-vendor resolver actually
10	//     trips in production (P2 #1 from the same review).
11	//
12	// Do NOT edit the prompt text without bumping EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION and
13	// logging the change in DECISIONS.md.
14	
15	import { z } from 'zod';
16	import { KNOWLEDGE_DOMAINS, ENTITY_TYPES } from '@oracle/shared';
17	
18	export const EXTRACTION_PROMPT_VERSION = '2.0.0';
19	
20	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
21	// Claim type taxonomy (spec 9.4 + Part 6).
22	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
23	export const CLAIM_TYPES = [
24	  'process_rule',      // How something normally works
25	  'exception_rule',    // An exception to a standard process
26	  'bottleneck',        // A constraint or delay point (person, system, or approval)
27	  'workaround',        // An unofficial solution for a missing capability
28	  'system_limitation', // What a system (Coldlion, ERP, etc.) fails to do
29	  'dependency',        // Something that must occur before something else
30	  'handoff_gap',       // Information lost between departments
31	  'contradiction',     // Two conflicting facts about the same process
32	  'process_ambiguity', // Unclear ownership or unclear when/how something happens
33	] as const;
34	
35	export type ClaimType = (typeof CLAIM_TYPES)[number];
36	
37	// Group chat semantic roles (spec 9.5).
38	export const SEMANTIC_ROLES = [
39	  'claim_stated',
40	  'claim_confirmed',
41	  'claim_challenged',
42	  'claim_refined',
43	  'exception_introduced',
44	  'process_ambiguity_revealed',
45	] as const;
46	
47	export type SemanticRole = (typeof SEMANTIC_ROLES)[number];
48	
49	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
50	// Zod output schema for generateObject calls.
51	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
52	
53	export const ExtractionEvidenceSchema = z.object({
54	  exactQuote: z
55	    .string()
56	    .min(5)
57	    .describe(
58	      'Verbatim substring from the source message — must appear character-for-character in the message content. Do not paraphrase.',
59	    ),
60	  sourceMessageId: z
61	    .string()
62	    .describe(
63	      'The ID of the message this quote was taken from. Must be one of the provided message IDs.',
64	    ),
65	  confidence: z
66	    .number()
67	    .int()
68	    .min(1)
69	    .max(10)
70	    .describe('Confidence that this quote supports the claim (1=uncertain, 10=certain).'),
71	});
72	
73	export const ExtractionGapSchema = z.object({
74	  questionToAsk: z
75	    .string()
76	    .min(10)
77	    .describe('A follow-up question that would resolve an uncertainty revealed in this segment.'),
78	  whyItMatters: z
79	    .string()
80	    .min(10)
81	    .describe('Why resolving this gap is operationally important for POP Creations / Spruce Line.'),
82	  priority: z.enum(['low', 'medium', 'high', 'urgent']),
83	});
84	
85	/**
86	 * R5.5 — Entity reference the model surfaced from the text. The validator
87	 * resolves these against the canonical entity registry; the licensor-vs-
88	 * vendor type-mismatch check fires here if the model proposes a known
89	 * licensor under `entity_type = 'vendor'`.
90	 */
91	export const ExtractionEntityProposalSchema = z.object({
92	  entityType: z
93	    .enum(ENTITY_TYPES as unknown as [string, ...string[]])
94	    .describe(
95	      'The kind of entity. licensor is for entertainment-IP rights holders (Disney, Marvel, Star Wars, Warner Bros, NBCUniversal) — NEVER use vendor for those. vendor is the residual bucket for non-customer non-factory non-licensor business partners.',
96	    ),
97	  rawString: z
98	    .string()
99	    .min(1)
100	    .max(255)
101	    .describe(
102	      'The entity name as it appears in the message (the validator resolves aliases against the canonical registry).',
103	    ),
104	});
105	
106	/**
107	 * R5.5 / P1 #2 — Sensitivity flags. STRICT mode: only flag content that is
108	 * clearly disciplinary, medical, compensation, or hostile interpersonal.
109	 * Operational mentions of named employees are NOT sensitive — e.g. "Jordan
110	 * handles licensing" is fine. The flag only fires when the content itself
111	 * describes personnel/HR/medical/conflict material that shouldn't live in
112	 * the operational knowledge graph.
113	 *
114	 * Setting any of these true causes the candidate to be quarantined and
115	 * never promoted into the `claims` table.
116	 */
117	export const ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema = z.object({
118	  containsSensitiveHRData: z
119	    .boolean()
120	    .describe(
121	      'True ONLY if the message contains: formal disciplinary action (warning, PIP, termination), compensation specifics (salary numbers, bonus amounts, raise discussions), formal performance reviews/ratings, or formally documented LOA reasons (medical leave, family leave). NOT true for casual operational mentions of who is on vacation, who handles a process, or who is out.',
122	    ),
123	  containsSensitivePersonalData: z
124	    .boolean()
125	    .describe(
126	      'True ONLY if the message contains explicit personal information: medical conditions or diagnoses, family situations (divorce, deaths, dependents), legal issues (lawsuits, arrests, immigration status), home addresses or personal contact details, or other clearly private personal facts unrelated to operational work.',
127	    ),
128	  isPersonalConflict: z
129	    .boolean()
130	    .describe(
131	      'True ONLY if the message describes explicit interpersonal hostility BETWEEN NAMED INDIVIDUALS: shouting matches, harassment accusations, character attacks, or formal complaints filed against another employee. NOT true for normal disagreement about a process or generic team frustration.',
132	    ),
133	  sensitivityReason: z
134	    .string()
135	    .max(500)
136	    .optional()
137	    .describe(
138	      'If any of the three flags is true, briefly explain which specific spans triggered the flag. Omit if all three are false.',
139	    ),
140	});
141	
142	export const ExtractionClaimSchema = z.object({
143	  claimType: z.enum(CLAIM_TYPES),
144	  summary: z
145	    .string()
146	    .min(10)
147	    .max(1000)
148	    .describe(
149	      'One declarative sentence stating what this claim asserts about company operations. Should be independently comprehensible without the source messages.',
150	    ),
151	  impactScore: z
152	    .number()
153	    .int()
154	    .min(1)
155	    .max(10)
156	    .describe('Business impact if this claim is true (1=trivial curiosity, 10=critical blocker).'),
157	  confidenceScore: z
158	    .number()
159	    .int()
160	    .min(1)
161	    .max(10)
162	    .describe(
163	      'How confident you are that this is a reliable, well-supported operational claim (1=speculative, 10=certain from explicit statement).',
164	    ),
165	  domains: z
166	    .array(z.enum(KNOWLEDGE_DOMAINS as unknown as [string, ...string[]]))
167	    .min(1)
168	    .describe('Which knowledge domains this claim belongs to (at least one required).'),
169	  evidence: ExtractionEvidenceSchema,
170	  semanticRole: z
171	    .enum(SEMANTIC_ROLES)
172	    .optional()
173	    .describe(
174	      'In group chats: how this claim was introduced. Omit for single-speaker segments.',
175	    ),
176	  requiresReview: z
177	    .boolean()
178	    .describe(
179	      'Set true if any of: impact >= 7, the claim names a specific person as a bottleneck, or the claim implies customer or licensor risk.',
180	    ),
181	  proposedEntities: z
182	    .array(ExtractionEntityProposalSchema)
183	    .optional()
184	    .describe(
185	      'Distinct entities (customers, licensors, factories, systems, departments, geographies, etc.) referenced in this claim. Omit if no proper-noun entities are mentioned.',
186	    ),
187	  sensitivityFlags: ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema.optional().describe(
188	    'Set when any of the three flags is true. Omit when nothing is sensitive (the common case for operational claims).',
189	  ),
190	  suggestedGaps: z
191	    .array(ExtractionGapSchema)
192	    .optional()
193	    .describe(
194	      'Follow-up questions that would resolve uncertainties or ambiguities revealed in this segment.',
195	    ),
196	});
197	
198	export const ExtractionOutputSchema = z.object({
199	  claims: z
200	    .array(ExtractionClaimSchema)
201	    .describe('All operational claims extracted from this conversation segment. Empty array if no claims found.'),
202	  segmentSummary: z
203	    .string()
204	    .max(2000)
205	    .optional()
206	    .describe(
207	      'Brief (1–2 sentence) summary of what this conversation segment covered operationally. Keep it concise — well under 2000 characters. Omit if the segment was trivial or off-topic.',
208	    ),
209	});
210	
211	export type ExtractionOutput = z.infer<typeof ExtractionOutputSchema>;
212	export type ExtractionClaim = z.infer<typeof ExtractionClaimSchema>;
213	export type ExtractionSensitivityFlags = z.infer<typeof ExtractionSensitivityFlagsSchema>;
214	export type ExtractionEntityProposal = z.infer<typeof ExtractionEntityProposalSchema>;
215	
216	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
217	// System prompt text.
218	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
219	
220	export const EXTRACTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are an operational knowledge extractor for The Oracle.
221	
222	The Oracle is an AI knowledge graph mapping the "dark matter" of POP Creations / Spruce Line — a high-volume home decor company (Brooklyn, NY). They design in-house, manufacture in China, and sell to Burlington, TJX, Ross, Hobby Lobby, Walmart, and others. They use an ERP system called Coldlion. They license from Disney, Marvel, Star Wars / Lucasfilm, Warner Bros, and NBCUniversal.
223	
224	YOUR TASK: Analyze the provided conversation segment and extract concrete, evidence-backed operational claims about how this company actually works. For each claim, also surface:
225	  (1) the entities it references (customers, licensors, factories, systems, departments, geographies),
226	  (2) whether the source content is sensitive in a way that should keep the claim OUT of the operational knowledge graph.
227	
228	CLAIM TYPES:
229	- process_rule: How something normally works ("We always send artwork to China after licensor approval")
230	- exception_rule: An exception to a standard process ("For Walmart seasonal, sourcing sees it earlier")
231	- bottleneck: A constraint, delay, or dependency on a specific person/system/approval
232	- workaround: An unofficial solution someone built because the official system is insufficient
233	- system_limitation: What a system (Coldlion, ERP, email, spreadsheet) fails to do or show
234	- dependency: Something that must occur before something else can proceed
235	- handoff_gap: Information that tends to get lost between departments or people
236	- contradiction: Two conflicting statements about the same process
237	- process_ambiguity: Unclear ownership, unclear when something happens, or unclear who is responsible
238	
239	GROUP CHAT SEMANTICS — track how claims interact when multiple employees speak:
240	- claim_stated: First assertion of a new fact
241	- claim_confirmed: Another employee agrees
242	- claim_challenged: Another employee disagrees
243	- claim_refined: A nuance or caveat is added to a prior claim
244	- exception_introduced: An exception to a stated rule is revealed
245	- process_ambiguity_revealed: The conversation reveals that employees have conflicting understandings
246	
247	EXTRACTION RULES:
248	1. Only extract claims supported by what employees actually said.
249	2. exactQuote MUST be a verbatim substring of the source message — never paraphrase.
250	3. sourceMessageId must be one of the message IDs provided — do not invent IDs.
251	4. Do NOT extract: pleasantries, greetings, Oracle assistant messages, or obvious generic facts with no operational specificity.
252	5. A single message may yield 0, 1, or multiple claims.
253	6. Set requiresReview=true if: impact >= 7, the claim names a specific person as a bottleneck, OR the claim implies customer or licensor risk.
254	7. Suggest gaps (follow-up questions) when the segment reveals uncertainty or ambiguity that needs resolution.
255	8. Do not flatten group conversation — if two employees express different views about the same process, extract both as separate claims with appropriate semantic roles and potentially a contradiction.
256	
257	ENTITY EXTRACTION RULES:
258	9. List every distinct entity the claim REFERENCES — not just the message-wide entities, but the ones THIS claim depends on.
259	10. The entityType values you may use are exactly: system, customer, licensor, factory, freight_provider, testing_lab, packaging_supplier, service_provider, vendor, person, sku_or_product_line, process_stage, department, geography, document_class.
260	11. \`licensor\` is reserved for entertainment / IP rights holders: Disney, Marvel, Star Wars, Lucasfilm, Warner Bros, NBCUniversal, etc. NEVER use \`vendor\` for those. \`vendor\` is the residual bucket for non-customer non-factory non-licensor business partners.
261	12. \`customer\` is the named retailer / buyer: Burlington, TJX, Ross, Hobby Lobby, Walmart, etc.
262	13. \`factory\` is an overseas manufacturer; \`freight_provider\` / \`testing_lab\` / \`packaging_supplier\` / \`service_provider\` are operational-vendor subtypes — pick the specific subtype if it fits, fall back to \`vendor\` only if none does.
263	14. \`system\` is software / tooling: Coldlion, ResourceSpace, Photoshop, Illustrator, Excel, Supabase, Email, WhatsApp, etc.
264	15. \`rawString\` should be how the entity appears in the message text — don't normalize to canonical form, the validator does that.
265	16. Omit \`proposedEntities\` entirely if the claim references no proper-noun entities.
266	
267	SENSITIVITY RULES — STRICT MODE:
268	17. Set \`sensitivityFlags\` ONLY when the content is clearly sensitive. Operational mentions of named people are NOT sensitive by themselves.
269	18. \`containsSensitiveHRData\` = true ONLY if the message describes: formal disciplinary actions (warnings, PIPs, terminations), compensation specifics (salary numbers, bonus amounts, raise discussions), formal performance reviews/ratings, or formally documented LOA reasons (medical leave, family leave). "Jordan is out next week" is NOT HR data. "We put Jordan on a final-written warning today" IS.
270	19. \`containsSensitivePersonalData\` = true ONLY for explicit personal information: medical conditions or diagnoses, family situations (divorce, deaths, dependents), legal issues, home addresses, personal contact details, or other clearly private personal facts.
271	20. \`isPersonalConflict\` = true ONLY for explicit interpersonal hostility BETWEEN NAMED INDIVIDUALS: shouting matches, harassment, character attacks, or formal complaints. Normal disagreement about a process is NOT a personal conflict.
272	21. If any flag fires, include a brief \`sensitivityReason\` naming which specific text triggered it. Omit \`sensitivityFlags\` entirely when nothing is sensitive — that's the common case.
273	22. When in doubt, do NOT set a flag. The cost of a false positive (admin reviews the quarantined candidate manually) is acceptable; the cost of a false negative (sensitive content reaches the knowledge graph) is not. But the threshold is "would a privacy/HR officer at this company call this sensitive?" — operational mentions of who works on what don't reach that bar.
274	
275	OUTPUT: Return only the structured JSON matching the schema. No narrative explanation outside the JSON.`;
276	
277	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
278	// Helper: format a conversation segment for the extraction model.
279	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
280	
281	export type FormattedMessage = {
282	  id: string;
283	  role: 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system';
284	  content: string;
285	  authorName: string | null;
286	  createdAt: Date;
287	};
288	
289	/**
290	 * Format a list of messages into a labeled conversation string for the extraction model.
291	 * Each message gets its ID, speaker, timestamp, and content clearly marked.
292	 */
293	export function formatConversationSegment(msgs: FormattedMessage[]): string {
294	  const lines: string[] = ['CONVERSATION SEGMENT:', '---'];
295	  for (const m of msgs) {
296	    if (m.role === 'system') continue;
297	    const speaker =
298	      m.role === 'assistant' ? '[Oracle — do not extract claims from this]' : `[${m.authorName ?? 'Unknown Employee'}]`;
299	    const timestamp = new Date(m.createdAt).toISOString().slice(0, 16).replace('T', ' ');
300	    lines.push(`Message ID: ${m.id}`);
301	    lines.push(`Speaker: ${speaker}`);
302	    lines.push(`Time: ${timestamp}`);
303	    lines.push(`Content: ${m.content}`);
304	    lines.push('---');

content.text:
I've now read the full AI/caching core directly and have three subagent reviews covering workers, adapters/routing, and retrieval/validation. I have what I need. Let me synthesize.

I made **no code changes** — this is findings only.

---

# Oracle codebase review — bugs, inefficiencies, and AI/caching gaps

## Headline: prompt caching is effectively **inert on the primary chat path**

This is the single most important finding, and it sits in the just-reworked [chat route](apps/web/app/api/chat/route.ts) + [anthropic-adapter.ts](packages/ai/src/providers/anthropic-adapter.ts). Two compounding problems mean the default interview path (`anthropic_claude_haiku_4_5_interview_primary`) pays full input price on essentially every turn:

**1. The system prompt is below the cache floor, so it's never cached. (HIGH)**
[oracle-system.ts:7](packages/ai/src/prompts/oracle-system.ts) is ~2.6 KB ≈ **~650 tokens**. The Anthropic adapter requires `ANTHROPIC_HAIKU_CACHE_MIN_TOKENS = 2048` ([anthropic-adapter.ts:68](packages/ai/src/providers/anthropic-adapter.ts), checked in `buildSystem` at line 312-314). Since `stableTokens (~650) < 2048`, `buildSystem` returns a plain string with **no `cache_control`**. So even the one thing the architecture caches — the stable system prompt — is never marked cacheable on the default route. (Anthropic genuinely won't cache <2048 tokens on Haiku, so the floor is correct; the fix is to *grow the cached stable prefix*, not lower the floor — see below.)

**2. The chat route puts volatile per-turn content *first* in the message array, busting conversation prefix-caching. (HIGH)**
[route.ts:428-436](apps/web/app/api/chat/route.ts) builds `messagesWithContext = [ {volatile dynamicContext}, ...safeMessages ]`. `dynamicContext` ([route.ts:194-212](apps/web/app/api/chat/route.ts)) contains the **freshly retrieved relevant claims** (re-retrieved from the latest message every turn) and open gaps — it changes every single turn. By placing it at position 0, right after the system prompt, the entire conversation history that follows it can never form a stable cacheable prefix across turns. The growing, genuinely-stable conversation history (the ideal cache target) is positioned *after* the volatile block. The breakpoint logic in [anthropic-adapter.ts:360-369](packages/ai/src/providers/anthropic-adapter.ts) marks the penultimate message, but it's moot — the prefix is already broken at index 1.

This is precisely the failure mode the `ContextCompiler` exists to prevent ([context-compiler.ts:110](packages/ai/src/context/context-compiler.ts) `assertStableBeforeDynamic`) — but the chat route bypasses the compiler's ordering by routing everything through `providerOptions.messages` and hand-ordering it dynamic-first.

**Fix for both:** Move the per-turn `dynamicContext` to the **end** of the message sequence (append to or place immediately before the latest user turn), so the layout is `[system][stable history…][volatile context + latest question]`. The conversation prefix then caches incrementally. To get the system prompt over the Haiku floor, fold the semi-stable domain/taxonomy context into the cached stable region so the cached prefix clears 2048 tokens. As-is, the entire `cache:{preferLongLivedCache, expectedReuseCount:6,…}` block passed at [route.ts:450-462](apps/web/app/api/chat/route.ts) buys nothing on the Anthropic path.

---

## Other AI / caching findings

**3. Vertex creates a fresh per-turn explicit cache for multi-turn chat. (MED)**
[vertex-gemini-adapter.ts:280-329](packages/ai/src/providers/vertex-gemini-adapter.ts): the non-file `prepareExplicitCacheForText` caches `contents.slice(0,-1)` (the whole prior conversation) with a `cacheKey` that includes `prefixText`. Every turn the conversation grows → different hash → a brand-new `caches.create` that's reused once and orphaned next turn. With `persistProviderCacheRecord` on ([route.ts:458](apps/web/app/api/chat/route.ts)), this also churns `provider_cached_content` rows. Explicit caching only pays off with reuse; for fast back-and-forth chat, implicit caching is cheaper. Gate explicit-cache creation on a *stable* prefix (system + retrieved context), not the whole conversation, or only for the file-backed PDF path.

**4. `generateObject` drops `providerOptions` in OpenAI and DeepSeek adapters. (HIGH)**
[openai-adapter.ts:114](packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts) and [deepseek-adapter.ts:120](packages/ai/src/providers/deepseek-adapter.ts) call `buildMessages(systemPrompt, userMessage)` with no `providerOptions`, silently discarding any multi-turn `messages` override and locking `temperature` at 0.1. Beyond correctness, a truncated single-message prompt won't match the stored multi-turn prefix, defeating DeepSeek/OpenAI prefix caching for structured calls. (Anthropic and Vertex thread it correctly.)

**5. Schema-validation failures are swallowed, neutralizing fallback. (MED–HIGH)**
All structured adapters do `object: (validated ?? parsed)` ([anthropic-adapter.ts:159](packages/ai/src/providers/anthropic-adapter.ts), [vertex:216](packages/ai/src/providers/vertex-gemini-adapter.ts), openai/deepseek/qwen). `tryZodParse` returns `null` both for "not a Zod schema" and "validation failed" ([vertex-gemini-adapter.ts:1015](packages/ai/src/providers/vertex-gemini-adapter.ts)), so a real validation failure is silently swallowed and unvalidated output flows through typed as valid. Because the adapter never throws, `ModelRouter.shouldFallback` ([model-router.ts:149](packages/ai/src/routing/model-router.ts)) never sees `schema_validation_failure`, so the documented schema-repair/fallback flow can't engage.

**6. Router fallback uses substring matching on error messages. (MED)**
[model-router.ts:152-155](packages/ai/src/routing/model-router.ts) matches `msg.includes('429' | 'timeout' | …)`. A validation error whose text contains "timeout", or a model response body containing "429", triggers an unintended provider fallback. Prefer typed status codes (`err.status === 429`, SDK `APIError` instances).

**7. Qwen cache-token normalization reads an Anthropic-shaped field. (MED)**
[qwen-adapter.ts](packages/ai/src/providers/qwen-adapter.ts) `normalizeUsage` reads `prompt_tokens_details.cache_creation_input_tokens` — that's the Anthropic shape; DashScope never returns it, so `cacheWriteTokens` is always empty, and the central [usage-normalizer.ts:66](packages/ai/src/usage/usage-normalizer.ts) routes Qwen through `normalizeOpenAI`, which doesn't understand the Responses-API `input_tokens_details` shape the adapter actually produces. Qwen cache accounting is effectively lost.

**8. `embedMany` ignores OpenAI's `index` ordering. (HIGH — silent data corruption)**
[embeddings.ts:82-87](packages/ai/src/embeddings.ts) does `body.data.map(d => d.embedding)` with no sort by `index`, no length check vs input, no per-vector dim check (unlike `embedText`). The embeddings API does not guarantee response order matches input order — vectors can be silently attached to the wrong claim/chunk. Sort by `d.index`, assert `data.length === texts.length`, and validate each length against `EMBEDDING_DIM`. Also no request batching ([embeddings.ts:71](packages/ai/src/embeddings.ts)) — a large backfill will 400.

**9. Cluster naming uses `runText` + regex JSON extraction instead of `runObject`. (MED)**
`taxonomy-reevaluation.ts` `nameCluster` parses `/\{[\s\S]*\}/` out of free text — the exact suboptimal-AI pattern the structured-output layer exists to replace. Use `client.runObject` with a Zod schema; also set `cacheEligible` on the stable naming system prompt (each cluster currently re-pays it).

---

## Concurrency / correctness bugs (workers)

**10. The per-hour interjection cap is not enforced under concurrency. (HIGH)**
`teams-live-recall-utterance.ts`, `lull-interjection.ts`, and `contradiction-watcher.ts` all read rate/cooldown state, then make a multi-second model call, then insert `oracleInterventions` — with no advisory lock, unique constraint, or re-check. Multiple utterances (and the minute-cron lull task overlapping itself or the other two writers on the same channel) all pass the `inLastHour < max` gate simultaneously and all post. Gate the read→draft→insert behind `pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(channelId))` with an in-lock re-read, or claim gaps atomically via `UPDATE … WHERE status='open' RETURNING`.

**11. Contradiction pair-dedup query is fragile. (MED–HIGH)**
`contradiction-watcher.ts` uses `claimAId IN [a,b] AND claimBId IN [a,b]` to test for an existing unordered pair — it can match self-pairs and is loose. Use the explicit `(claimAId=a AND claimBId=b) OR (claimAId=b AND claimBId=a)`.

**12. `detectionConfidence` is hardcoded to the threshold. (MED)**
`contradiction-watcher.ts` passes `detectionConfidence: CONTRADICTION_LIVE_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD`, so the "confidence ≥ threshold" gate is a tautology and the stored value is meaningless. Either add a numeric confidence to the adjudication schema or drop the field.

**13. Document quote→chunk matching silently rejects valid claims. (MED)**
`document-ingestion.ts` picks the evidence chunk via `chunk.text.includes(exactQuote)`; chunks overlap by 150 chars, so a quote spanning a boundary matches no chunk, falls back to `insertedChunkIds[0]`, then fails quote validation against the wrong chunk — the claim is rejected though the quote is genuinely in the doc. Match against full `rawText` and map the offset back to the covering chunk.

**14. Invalid SQL in entity-proposal merge. (HIGH)**
`packages/oracle-engines/src/extraction/stage-entity-proposal.ts` uses `${rawString} = ANY(SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(...))` — not valid Postgres and a divergence from the repo's endorsed `IN (SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(...))` pattern (per AGENTS.md §"Raw-SQL list parameters"). Likely untested against a real DB.

**15. `creative_design` excluded from operations-systems retrieval, and two domains are uninferable. (MED)**
[retrieval-plan.ts](packages/ai/src/retrieval-plan.ts): `inferTopDomainExclusions` excludes `creative_design` (a real domain in [domains.ts](packages/shared/src/domains.ts)) for operations queries, silently dropping its claims; meanwhile `creative_design` and `vendor_management` have **no `DOMAIN_KEYWORDS` entry**, so queries about them fall to `global_fallback` (full-corpus noise). Add keyword groups or document the intent.

**16. Non-transactional multi-write sequences. (MED)**
`brain-synthesis.ts` updates `currentVersionId` then applies `sectionClaims`/gaps as separate statements (header claims "transactional" but isn't); `taxonomy-reclassification.ts` `applyProposal` handlers move/delete/retire across statements with no transaction and no change-log row on partial failure. Wrap each in `db.transaction`.

**17. `claim-extraction.ts` orphans messages stuck in `'processing'`. (MED)**
Messages are flipped to `'processing'` ([~line 196]) with no reaper; a crash mid-run leaves them permanently un-extractable (the cron only selects `'pending'`). Add a stale-`processing` reset, mirroring document-ingestion.

---

## Lower-severity / efficiency (condensed)

| # | File | Issue | Sev |
|---|---|---|---|
| 18 | recall.ts | Transient `send_chat_message` failure re-throws → Trigger retry → dedup skip → interjection silently dropped | MED |
| 19 | lull-interjection.ts:184-289 | 6 sequential queries per channel × sequential channels; hoist global open-gap fetch out of loop, parallelize | MED |
| 20 | retrieval.ts:372/457 | Hybrid vs fallback `distance` on non-comparable scales (`1 - ts_rank` can go negative); any threshold caller diverges dev↔prod | MED |
| 21 | document-ingestion.ts | Chunk inserts are N+1; temp file leaks if `processDocument` throws pre-try-block (wrap in `finally`) | MED |
| 22 | evidence-validator.ts:53-70 | Offset path doesn't bound-check `start/end` (weaker than the engine's `quote-validator.ts`); dead or strictly-weaker validator | MED |
| 23 | taxonomy-reevaluation.ts | `Math.random()` k-means seed → non-deterministic proposals; no dedupe vs pending proposals | MED |
| 24 | claim-extraction-batch-drain.ts:305 | `promptVersion: undefined` on batch `model_runs` rows (sync path sets it) | LOW |
| 25 | openai-adapter.ts:364-397 | Comment says it strips `format`; `walk` only strips `$schema`/`default` | LOW |
| 26 | model-capabilities/index.ts:106 | Comment "3 provider APIs" but 5 are fetched; `$15.01` magic ceiling | LOW |
| 27 | trigger.config.ts | `maxAttempts:3` applies to per-minute cron + live tasks where retry is harmful | LOW |
| 28 | brain-synthesis.ts:581 | `resolvedGaps` update doesn't constrain `sectionId`/status — model can resolve any gap by UUID | LOW |
| 29 | diff-validator.ts:332 | Entity-support check: substring for summaries vs exact-match for registry — asymmetric, spurious holds | LOW |

---

## What's genuinely well done
The caching *architecture* is sound: `ContextCompiler` enforces stable-before-dynamic and computes per-region hashes ([context-compiler.ts](packages/ai/src/context/context-compiler.ts)); `cache-utils.ts` centralizes TTL/retention policy and static-first flattening; the Vertex explicit-cache lifecycle (Postgres-tracked, cross-process reuse by `source_hash`, sweeper cleanup) is thorough; the dual retrieval branches share `buildPlanMetadataFilters` with a CI parity guard. The problem is that the **chat route bypasses the compiler's ordering** and the **default model is below the cache floor**, so the well-built machinery doesn't engage where it matters most.

---

Want me to fix the top items? I'd suggest starting with the highest-leverage, lowest-risk set: **#2 (reorder chat context to the end)**, **#8 (embedMany ordering — silent corruption)**, and **#4 (thread providerOptions through generateObject)**. I can do those as a focused PR-ready change, or work through the full list. Which would you like?

input.prompt:
You are fixing reviewed bugs in the AI provider/routing layer of the repo at C:\repos\oracle. Make MINIMAL, conservative edits — only what each finding requires. Read each file and its surrounding code before editing. Do NOT commit. Do NOT touch files outside this list. After editing, run a typecheck for the package (`pnpm --filter @oracle/ai typecheck` if it exists, else `pnpm --filter @oracle/ai exec tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit`) and report what you ran and the result.

IMPORTANT context: All inference goes through these raw provider adapters. `flattenPlan`, `tryZodParse`, `zodToJsonSchema`, `parseJsonOrRaw` live in packages/ai/src/providers/vertex-gemini-adapter.ts and are imported by the other adapters. `cache-utils.ts` is shared. Preserve documented intentional behavior.

FILES + FIXES:

1. packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts — `generateObject` calls `this.buildMessages(systemPrompt, userMessage)` WITHOUT providerOptions (around line 114), discarding any multi-turn `messages` override and locking temperature at 0.1. Fix: thread `providerOptions` into buildMessages here exactly like `generateText` does (line ~75), and honor `providerOptions.temperature` falling back to 0.1.

2. packages/ai/src/providers/deepseek-adapter.ts — same bug: `generateObject` (around line 113-120) destructures `{ plan, route, schema }` omitting `providerOptions` and calls `buildMessages(systemPrompt, userMessage)`. Fix: accept and pass `providerOptions`, honor `providerOptions.temperature` (fallback 0.1).

3. tryZodParse swallowed-validation fix (packages/ai/src/providers/vertex-gemini-adapter.ts `tryZodParse`, lines ~1015-1023): it returns null BOTH when the value isn't a Zod schema AND when validation fails, so every adapter's `object: validated ?? parsed` silently returns unvalidated output and the router never sees a schema failure. Fix WITHOUT breaking the "not a zod schema" passthrough: change `tryZodParse` to THROW on a real Zod validation failure (when safeParse runs and fails), and return null ONLY when the input isn't a zod schema. Update its doc comment. Then verify every caller (anthropic-adapter generateObject line ~157, vertex generateObject line ~214, openai, deepseek, qwen generateObject) still compiles — they currently do `const validated = tryZodParse(...); return { object: validated ?? parsed }`. Keep that shape (a thrown validation error now propagates so ModelRouter.shouldFallback can act). Do NOT add a new catch that swallows it.

4. packages/ai/src/routing/model-router.ts — `shouldFallback` (lines 149-158) matches error MESSAGE substrings ('429','timeout',etc.), risking false fallbacks. Improve: before the substring checks, check typed status when available — if the error object has a numeric `status` (or `statusCode`) === 429 or >=500, return true; keep the existing substring checks as a fallback. Use a safe typeof/`in` guard (errors are `unknown`). Don't remove existing behavior.

5. packages/ai/src/providers/qwen-adapter.ts — `normalizeUsage` reads `prompt_tokens_details.cache_creation_input_tokens` (an Anthropic-shaped field DashScope never returns) into cacheWriteTokens — always undefined/dead. Read the file's two usage normalizers (chat `normalizeUsage` and `normalizeResponseUsage`). Fix: remove the bogus Anthropic-shaped read; ensure the Responses-API path (`normalizeResponseUsage`) correctly reads `input_tokens_details.cached_tokens` into cachedInputTokens (keep if already correct). Add a brief comment that DashScope does not report cache-creation tokens. Do not invent field names.

6. packages/ai/src/usage/usage-normalizer.ts — the Qwen branch routes through `normalizeOpenAI` which doesn't understand the Responses-API `input_tokens_details` shape. Make the qwen branch handle BOTH the Chat (`prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens`) and Responses (`input_tokens_details.cached_tokens`) usage shapes for cachedInputTokens. Also there's a duplicate `DeepSeekUsageRaw` type here and in deepseek-adapter.ts — leave the dedup alone unless trivial. Add a `default: throw` to the provider switch (lines ~56-68) for runtime exhaustiveness if it has none.

7. packages/ai/src/providers/openai-adapter.ts — the doc comment for `stripIncompatibleFields` (line ~364) claims it strips `format`, but `walk` only deletes `$schema` and `default`. Fix the COMMENT to match the code (do not change behavior).

8. packages/ai/src/embeddings.ts — `embedMany` (lines ~71-87) does `body.data.map(d => d.embedding)` with NO sort by `index`, no length check, no per-vector dim check. The OpenAI embeddings API does not guarantee response order. Fix: add `index: number` to the embedding response type; sort `body.data` by `index` ascending; assert `data.length === texts.length` (throw with a clear message otherwise); validate each vector length === EMBEDDING_DIM (reuse the same check `embedText` uses). Also add request batching: if `texts.length` exceeds a safe cap (use 256), split into chunks, call the endpoint per chunk, and concatenate in order. Keep `embedText` behavior unchanged.

9. packages/ai/src/model-capabilities/index.ts — fix the stale inner comment "all 3 provider APIs" (line ~106) to say 5; replace the magic `15.01` price ceiling (line ~197) with a named const (e.g. `MAX_INPUT_PRICE_PER_M = 15.01`) and use it. Behavior unchanged.

Report a concise per-file summary of what you changed and the typecheck result.

You are fixing reviewed bugs in the retrieval / validation / oracle-engines layer of the repo at C:\repos\oracle. Make MINIMAL, conservative edits. Read each file and surrounding code before editing. Do NOT commit. Do NOT touch files outside this list (in particular do NOT edit packages/ai/src/embeddings.ts or any provider adapter — another agent owns those). After editing, run typecheck for the affected packages (`pnpm --filter @oracle/ai typecheck` and `pnpm --filter @oracle/engines typecheck`, or the tsc equivalent if no such script) and report what you ran + results.

PRESERVE these documented-intentional patterns (do NOT "fix" them): raw-SQL list params bound as JSON strings via `IN (SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(...))`; EMBEDDING_DIM inlined via sql.raw; the two retrieval SQL branches (hybrid + tsvector fallback) intentionally both build WHERE from buildPlanMetadataFilters.

FILES + FIXES:

1. packages/oracle-engines/src/extraction/stage-entity-proposal.ts (lines ~54-58) — uses `${args.rawString} = ANY(SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(raw_strings_observed))`, which is invalid Postgres and diverges from the repo convention. Fix to the endorsed form: `${args.rawString} IN (SELECT jsonb_array_elements_text(raw_strings_observed))`. Verify there are no other `= ANY(SELECT ...)` occurrences in this file; fix them the same way if present.

2. packages/ai/src/retrieval-plan.ts — `DOMAIN_KEYWORDS` (lines ~110-373) has no entry for two real top-level domains: `creative_design` and `vendor_management` (both defined in packages/shared/src/domains.ts), so queries about them fall to global_fallback. ADD reasonable keyword groups for both (look at how the existing groups are shaped and at the domain descriptions in domains.ts / AGENTS.md to choose keywords — e.g. creative_design: art, artwork, design concept, illustration, mockup, color, print design, packaging art; vendor_management: vendor management, vendor onboarding, vendor scorecard, vendor audit, supplier evaluation — but note vendor-management-adjacent terms currently sit under supply_chain, so choose terms that disambiguate). Then in `inferTopDomainExclusions` (line ~618-653) the operations-systems branch excludes `creative_design` (a real domain) which silently drops its claims — add a one-line comment explaining the intentional exclusion now that creative_design is inferable, OR if it reads as accidental, remove `creative_design` from that exclusion list. Use your judgment and state which you chose and why in your report. Do NOT change the JSON-string filter mechanics.

3. packages/ai/src/retrieval.ts — the tsvector fallback path sets `distance = 1 - ts_score` (line ~457) which can go negative and is on a non-comparable scale vs the hybrid cosine distance (line ~372). Fix: clamp the fallback to `GREATEST(0, 1 - ts_score)` in the SQL (or Math.max(0, ...) if computed in JS) so it can't be negative, and add a short code comment that `distance` is path-dependent and must not be cross-thresholded. Do not alter the WHERE/filter logic or the hybrid path ranking.

4. packages/ai/src/validation/evidence-validator.ts (lines ~53-70) — the offset-match path checks `slice === exactQuote` but doesn't validate offset sanity. Add guards BEFORE slicing: require both charStart and charEnd are numbers (reject if exactly one is provided), require charStart >= 0, charStart <= charEnd, charEnd <= sourceText.length; if any fails, fall through to / return the non-offset scan path result as appropriate (mirror the stricter checks already in packages/oracle-engines/src/extraction/quote-validator.ts lines ~67-103). Keep the existing scan-path behavior intact.

5. packages/oracle-engines/src/extraction/quote-validator.ts — `validateSourcePointer` switch (lines ~246-286) has no `default`, so an unexpected sourceType returns undefined and violates the return type. Add a `default` branch returning a failed result like `{ ok: false, failedCheckName: 'source_type_valid', detail: \`unknown source type: ${sourceType}\` }` (match the existing SourcePointerValidationResult shape exactly — read the type first).

6. packages/oracle-engines/src/synthesis/diff-validator.ts (lines ~332-335) — entity-support check uses substring match for summaries but exact-equality for the registry canonicals, an asymmetry causing spurious holds (e.g. "Walt Disney Company" vs registry "walt disney"). Make the registry side also use a token/substring-aware match consistent with the summary side (e.g. also accept when a registry canonical is a substring of the candidate phrase or vice-versa). Keep it conservative — this only affects whether synthesis is held for review; do not make it accept fabricated entities wholesale. Add a brief comment. If you judge this too risky to change behaviorally, instead just add a clear comment documenting the asymmetry and leave logic as-is — state your choice in the report.

Report a concise per-file summary + typecheck result.

You are fixing reviewed bugs in the Trigger.dev workers of the repo at C:\repos\oracle (all under apps/workers/). Make MINIMAL, conservative edits. Read each file and surrounding code before editing. Do NOT commit. Do NOT touch files outside apps/workers/. After editing, run `pnpm --filter @oracle/workers typecheck` (or the tsc equivalent if no such script — inspect apps/workers/package.json) and report what you ran + result.

Notes: advisory-lock fixes are CODE-ONLY (use drizzle raw `sql` with `pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext($key))` inside a `db.transaction`) — no DB migration. Preserve the two-phase batch design and the every-tick settings short-circuit gates documented in AGENTS.md. Use drizzle `sql` template; remember raw list params follow the JSON-string + jsonb_array_elements_text convention.

FILES + FIXES:

1. CONCURRENCY (highest priority) — the per-hour interjection cap is not enforced under concurrency in three workers that all write `oracleInterventions` per channel: apps/workers/src/trigger/teams-live-recall-utterance.ts, lull-interjection.ts, contradiction-watcher.ts. Each reads rate/cooldown state, then makes a multi-second model call, then inserts the intervention with NO lock or re-check. Fix pattern for each: wrap the decision-commit sequence (the part that re-checks rate/cooldown state and inserts the oracleInterventions row + posts) in a `db.transaction(async (tx) => { ... })` that FIRST acquires `pg_try_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(<channelId>))`; if the lock is not acquired, skip posting (return a 'skipped: lock_contended' style result); after acquiring, RE-READ the rate/cooldown counts inside the tx and only insert+post if still under cap/cooldown. For lull-interjection, also claim the gap atomically: `UPDATE gaps SET status='asked' WHERE id=? AND status='open' RETURNING` before drafting, and skip if no row returned. Keep the model call OUTSIDE the transaction where possible (don't hold the lock across the LLM call if the existing structure allows re-checking after) — but the re-check + insert MUST be inside the lock. Read each file carefully; the three have different structures. Be precise and preserve existing return shapes/logging.

2. contradiction-watcher.ts — the pair-existence dedup query uses `claimAId IN [a,b] AND claimBId IN [a,b]`. Replace with the explicit unordered-pair check: `(claimAId = a AND claimBId = b) OR (claimAId = b AND claimBId = a)` (use drizzle `or(and(eq,eq), and(eq,eq))`).

3. contradiction-watcher.ts — `detectionConfidence` is passed as the threshold constant itself (tautological gate). Add an optional numeric `confidence` (1-10 or 0-1, match existing scale conventions in the file) to the contradiction adjudication Zod schema, instruct it briefly in the schema `.describe()`, thread the model's returned confidence through to the gate and into the stored `contradictions.detectionConfidence`. If the model omits it, fall back to current behavior. Keep severity handling.

4. document-ingestion.ts — evidence chunk is chosen via `chunk.text.includes(exactQuote)` which fails for quotes spanning the 150-char chunk overlap, silently falling back to chunk[0] and then failing quote validation. Fix: search the full rawText for the quote; if found, map the match offset to the chunk whose [start,end] covers it (chunks are inserted in order with known offsets — inspect how chunks are created) and attach THAT chunk as the evidence pointer; only fall back to chunk[0] if the quote truly isn't in rawText. Keep existing validation downstream.

5. document-ingestion.ts — the materialized temp file (fileBackedCachePath, created ~line 195) leaks if processing throws before the existing unlink paths. Wrap the doc-processing body so the temp file is unlinked in a `finally`. Also the per-chunk INSERT loop (~lines 251-275) is N+1 — batch into a single multi-row insert with `onConflictDoNothing` collecting returned ids, preserving order. Be careful to keep returned-id ordering aligned with chunks for the embedding/evidence mapping.

6. claim-extraction.ts — messages flipped to `'processing'` are never reset on a crash (only 'pending' is re-selected). At the TOP of the scheduled task, before selecting pending work, add a reaper: `UPDATE messages SET extraction_status='pending' WHERE extraction_status='processing' AND <updated/created timestamp> < now() - interval '2 hours'` (use the appropriate timestamp column — inspect schema usage in the file). Keep it idempotent and logged.

7. brain-synthesis.ts — version-insert + currentVersionId update + sectionClaims inserts + gap insert/resolve run as separate un-transactioned statements (~lines 550-586). Wrap them in a single `db.transaction`. Also batch the sectionClaims inserts (single multi-row `values([...])`) and resolve gaps with one `inArray` update instead of N+1 loops. And the gap-resolve update must constrain to this section + open status (add `AND sectionId=? AND status='open'`-style guard) so the model can't resolve arbitrary gaps by UUID.

8. taxonomy-reclassification.ts — each `applyProposal` handler runs multiple dependent mutations with no transaction and no change-log row on partial failure. Wrap each handler body in `db.transaction` so the move/delete/retire + the taxonomy_change_log 'applied' row commit atomically. Don't change the proposal-selection logic.

9. taxonomy-reevaluation.ts — `nameCluster` parses JSON from `runText` output via a `/\{[\s\S]*\}/` regex. Convert to a structured-output call: use the OracleAIClient `runObject` with a small Zod schema `{ name: string, purpose: string }` (mirror how other workers call runObject). Also set `cacheEligible: true` on the stable system block of the naming prompt (use makeBlock's cacheEligible). Additionally, dedupe newly-proposed clusters against EXISTING PENDING create_sub_topic proposals (not only against knowledgeSubTopics) before inserting. If runObject isn't readily available in this worker, match the import pattern used by contradiction-watcher/lull-interjection.

10. recall.ts (apps/workers/src/lib or trigger) — `sendRecallChatMessage` has no retry/error-class handling; a transient send failure re-throws and (via Trigger retry + the utterance dedup) silently drops the interjection. In teams-live-recall-utterance.ts, wrap the send so a transient send failure is logged and the task COMPLETES (does not re-throw) after the utterance message is already persisted — so it isn't retried into a dedup-skip. Be careful: only swallow the SEND error, not earlier errors.

11. trigger.config.ts — `retries.default.maxAttempts: 3` applies to per-minute cron + live tasks where re-execution is harmful. Set `maxAttempts: 1` (retries disabled) specifically on the cron/live tasks: lull-interjection, teams-live-recall-utterance, and any other scheduled cron task — set it per-task via the task's `retry` option rather than changing the global default, so batch/ingestion tasks keep retries. Inspect each task definition to add the per-task override.

If any fix needs a DB column that doesn't exist (e.g. #3 confidence is already a column per AGENTS.md — verify), do NOT create migrations; adapt to existing columns and note it. Report a concise per-file summary + typecheck result. If any item is too risky/ambiguous to change safely, leave it, add a TODO comment, and flag it clearly in your report rather than guessing.
